name: Alara
classification_notes: The specimen shows a clean, upright sans-serif with humanist touches across a wide weight range; the overall texture and letter forms suggest a contemporary grotesque lineage.
summary: A six-weight grotesque sans from Proof of Words, each weight accompanied by an italic, spanning Light through Black.
description: Twelve styles in total, running from Light to Black with matching italics throughout. The specimen suggests a clean, readable grotesque suited to editorial and screen use across a broad range of settings. Little contextual information is given about the design's origins or intent, but the weight range offers practical flexibility for text and display work alike.
foundry_blurb: Proof of Words present Alara as a typeface available in twelve styles — Light through Black, each with a corresponding italic — demonstrating the family across both display and text sizes.

name: Garnier
classification_notes: The specimen shows bracketed serifs, moderate contrast between thick and thin strokes, and upright italic forms consistent with a transitional serif classification. The range from Light to ExtraBold with italics suggests a versatile text and display family in the transitional tradition.
summary: A transitional serif family from Proof of Words spanning five weights with matching italics, suited to text and editorial work.
description: Five weights from Light to ExtraBold, each with a companion italic, give Garnier a range broad enough for both running text and display use. The specimen shows confident stroke contrast and well-proportioned letterforms consistent with the transitional model. A practical choice for editorial settings where a conventional serif is needed without eccentricity.
foundry_blurb: Proof of Words offer Garnier as a serif family in ten styles, running from Light through ExtraBold with italic companions at every weight.

name: Galmi
classification_notes: Galmi presents clean, upright letterforms with a neutral grotesque character, showing no obvious humanist pen influence or geometric construction — placing it firmly in the grotesque sans-serif tradition.
summary: A five-weight grotesque sans from Proof of Words, suited to text-heavy editorial and long-form reading contexts.
description: Spanning Regular through Black across five weights, it handles extended prose and display-scale settings with equal composure. The specimen draws on passages from Audubon, Einstein and Kant — texts chosen to stress the face at paragraph length and demonstrate its legibility under density. No italics or condensed cuts are listed, keeping the family lean and purposeful.
foundry_blurb: Proof of Words present Galmi as a grotesque sans available in five weights from Regular to Black, demonstrated through long-form philosophical and scientific texts to show its range across sizes and typographic contexts.

name: Covenant
classification_notes: The specimen text and weight range suggest a text-oriented serif with classical proportions; the broad weight range from ExtraLight to Black with italics throughout is consistent with a transitional or humanist serif designed for both text and display use.
summary: A text serif from Proof of Words spanning seven weights from ExtraLight to Black, each with a matching italic.
description: Fourteen styles run across seven weights, covering ExtraLight through to Black with corresponding italics throughout. The specimen shows the family performing capably at small text sizes and at large display scale, suggesting a design intended for continuous reading as much as headline use. The weight range gives it flexibility across editorial, book and identity applications.
foundry_blurb: Proof of Words present Covenant as a fourteen-style serif family running from ExtraLight to Black, with italic companions at every weight, suitable for a wide range of typographic applications.

name: Limerick
classification_notes: Limerick is an ornamental display family built around decorative geometric shape variants — Clover, Mosaic, Star, Lozenge, Octogone, Square, Circle, Flower — placing it firmly in the display/ornamental category with no conventional text application.
summary: A modular ornamental display family from Proof of Words, built across eight geometric sub-styles including Clover, Mosaic, Star and Circle.
description: Each sub-family within Limerick applies a distinct geometric shape motif to the letterforms, producing a system of eight decorative variants that share a common underlying structure. The range spans Clover, Mosaic, Star, Lozenge, Octogone, Square, Circle and Flower, giving designers a broad palette of ornamental registers within a single family. Suited to logotypes, editorial headers and decorative contexts where geometric patterning is the intended effect.
foundry_blurb: Proof of Words present Limerick as a family of eight ornamental display typefaces, each named after and shaped by a distinct geometric motif: Clover, Mosaic, Star, Lozenge, Octogone, Square, Circle and Flower.

name: Covenant Mono
classification_notes: A fixed-width typeface offered across multiple weights with italics, designed for code, screen and extended text settings in the monospaced tradition.
summary: A five-weight monospaced family with italics throughout, suited to code environments and screen-set extended text.
description: Ten styles span ExtraLight to Bold, each with a matching italic, giving Covenant Mono a weight range broader than most monospaced releases. The specimen demonstrates it at display sizes down to 16 px running body copy, suggesting the family is intended for general reading as much as terminal or code use. Proof of Words position it as a practical workhorse rather than a niche coding tool.
foundry_blurb: Proof of Words present Covenant Mono as a monospaced typeface available in five weights from ExtraLight to Bold, each accompanied by an italic, with specimens showing use from large display sizes down to small body text settings.

name: Savon
classification_notes: The page shows a text-weight serif with a companion italic, suggesting a traditional book or text serif design. The sample text usage and weight naming (Regular, Italic) point toward a transitional or humanist serif classification.
summary: A two-style text serif from Proof of Words, offered in Regular and Italic for body copy and editorial use.
description: Savon comes in two styles: Regular and Italic, designed for sustained reading at text sizes. The sample text on the page is set at length in both cuts, suggesting the family is intended primarily for body copy rather than display work. Limited weight range keeps it focused on a narrow but specific use case.
foundry_blurb: Proof of Words present Savon in Regular and Italic, with specimen text set at running length to demonstrate its performance as a text typeface.
